behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: failure is at the handler->queue boundary (must both return 401 and avoid forwarding), not just HMAC math. REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: no real OS/network/subprocess is required; in-process HTTP handler + queue spy catches it. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text only requires response code and forwarding suppression, which is fully testable in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Run the webhook handler in-process with a real signature-verification module and a mocked/spied queue adapter. Send a request with a mismatched `X-Signature-256` for the raw body. Assert HTTP 401 and assert queue `enqueue` was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this is an ordering guarantee across modules (HTTP handler and queue write). REAL-INFRA: no real network/subprocess required; ordering can be proven with in-process wiring and controlled async queue write. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text is about sequencing relative to `HTTP 200`, not external network behavior; in-process request/response timing is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process HTTP test harness with a queue implementation (or adapter) whose `enqueue` promise is controllable. Verify response is not completed before `enqueue` resolves, then resolves to 200 after successful write.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: requires module interaction between handler enqueue path and downstream worker lifecycle. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess needed; a never-resolving downstream processing promise can reproduce failure. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text is specifically about not awaiting downstream completion, which is testable entirely in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler with queue + downstream processor where processor intentionally blocks (e.g., unresolved promise). Send valid webhook and assert response completes successfully while processor is still running.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: REAL-INFRA/MOCK-HIDE: this is explicit wall-clock and concurrency behavior under load; in-process mocks/fake timers can hide event-loop starvation, scheduler contention, and real server latency. Failure boundary is runtime/OS scheduling plus real HTTP handling. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: Integration was considered, but behavior text explicitly requires `within 3 seconds wall-clock` under `slow and concurrent` conditions, which disqualifies purely mocked timing.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real Node HTTP server, seed concurrent slow downstream work from earlier events, then send new valid webhook requests over real HTTP and measure end-to-end latency with wall clock. Assert each response is returned in <3s despite concurrent backlog.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: dedupe correctness appears at boundary between request handling and persisted/event-id tracking state. Unit test of a helper is insufficient for real duplicate-write failure. REAL-INFRA: can be caught with local in-process persistence fixture; no OS/network/subprocess dependency required. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text only requires duplicate suppression on second receive, testable via module wiring and local state store.
behaviors.test_description: Send two webhook requests with the same event ID through the handler against a test queue/store. Assert first request writes once, second request does not create a second queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: behavior is mapping from queue backpressure signal to HTTP response contract (503 + header), requiring handler<->queue interaction. REAL-INFRA: no real OS/network/subprocess needed; queue-full condition can be simulated by queue module/adapter. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text is about response mapping to a queue status, not real network fault characteristics.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process handler with queue adapter configured to return `full`/capacity error. Send valid request and assert HTTP 503 and `Retry-After` header present with expected value format.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: this validates queue implementation plus persistence/config wiring, not just handler logic. REAL-INFRA: requires real queue storage behavior (e.g., local DB/filesystem), but still local integration; no subprocess/real external network needed. LLM-DEP/MULTI-STEP: none. Rejection gate: System was considered, but behavior text targets queue capacity enforcement itself and can be validated with local persistent backing store in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue with a small configured capacity in a test environment (local DB/file). Enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one more and assert rejection/error signaling for over-capacity.
